label: Fire & Emergency Management
entity_examples: County OES/OEM, city emergency management, fire districts, regional EM councils

Dept / Agency Profile: Counties and cities with populations over 50k
High-risk environmental zones (wildfire, flood, hurricane, flood plain)
Agencies facing expiring legacy alert contracts (CodeRED, Everbridge, Rave)
Regions with recent disasters or near-miss evacuation events
Multi-agency jurisdictions requiring shared common operating picture
Areas with known cellular vulnerability (mountains, coastal zones)
Mindset: Shaken by near-misses, understands what slow notification costs
Frustrated with current tools: drawing evacuation zones manually is unacceptable under pressure
Values inter-agency coordination over individual department heroics
Forward-thinking: plans for the worst-case scenario, not the average one
Politically exposed, knows a failed evacuation ends careers
Technology: Active GIS/CAD environment, open to integrations
Has or is exploring IPAWS integration for WEA and EAS
Recognizes cellular failure as a real scenario during major disasters
Open to cloud-native SaaS with offline/acoustic backup capability
Interested in sensor integration (weather, seismic, flood gauge)
Outcomes Desired: Evacuation speed: decision to public notification in under 10 minutes
Precision targeting - alert only those in harm's way, no gridlock from over-alerting
100% population reach including visitors and those without smartphones
Unified operating picture shared across Police, Fire, EOC, and Public Works
Clear evacuation routing via integration with Waze/Google Maps
Backup reach when power and cellular fail (acoustic integration)

caution_signals: Very small municipalities (<10k pop) with no dedicated EM staff or budget
Locked into long-term (5+ year) vendor deals with no exit clause
Leadership satisfied with tone-based sirens as only warning system
'We don't have wildfires / floods here' — low perceived risk
'Check the box' mentality - satisfies FEMA requirement, moves on
Resistant to cloud-based tools due to legacy IT department influence
Believes county GIS staff can replicate Genasys capabilities in-house
Heavy reliance on on-premise legacy hardware with IT gatekeeping
No budget line item for technology - relies on reactive grant cycles
Only wants the lowest-cost alerting tool to satisfy a mandate
decision_makers: Fire Chief (if EM sits under fire)

label: Law Enforcement
entity_examples: Police departments, sheriff's offices, SWAT/regional task forces, state law enforcement

Dept / Agency Profile: Police departments and sheriff's offices in communities over 25k
Multi-agency regions with active task forces or shared investigations
Agencies with known FOIA exposure from current consumer app use
Agencies in states with strict CJIS and public records compliance mandates
Departments modernizing from radio-only to mobile-first communications
Any SWAT department or agency with regular high-risk operations
Mindset: Compliance-focused (understands FOIA and CJIS are existential risks, not formalities)
Tired of the 'two phone' problem: personal apps for actual work, official tools that lag
Values speed of information over process, needs intel to flow in real time
Forward-thinking chief who wants progressive department reputation
Aware that a single FOIA violation or leaked WhatsApp chat can dominate the news cycle
Technology: Strict CJIS and FIPS 140-2 compliance mandate, non-negotiable
Mobile-first: officers need tools that work on phones in the field
CAD integration valued for real-time incident-linked communications
IT environment that can support cloud-hosted, FedRAMP-aligned tools
Interested in reducing app sprawl - wants fewer, better tools
Outcomes Desired: Full FOIA and CJIS compliance, every message auditable and retrievable
Real-time intel sharing: suspect photos reach every officer in under 60 seconds
Cross-agency coordination in shared Regional Rooms without separate logins
Urgent department-wide messaging that bypasses radio congestion
Reduced administrative overhead: shift briefings, BOLOs, scheduling in one tool
No data stored on personal devices, officers protected from e-discovery

caution_signals: Agencies under 10k population with no IT budget or dedicated tech staff
Leadership that views digital communication as a liability, not an asset
Departments locked into city-mandated enterprise tools (Teams, etc.)
Heavily unionized agencies where app adoption requires contract negotiation
IT department actively blocking any cloud or SaaS tools
Outdated MDT environment incompatible with modern mobile apps
Relies on records management system (RMS) vendor for all communications - vendor lock-in
Looking only for a cheap broadcast alert tool - not interested in collaboration
Wants a system that requires zero behavior change from officers
Will not pay per-user pricing; expects 'free like WhatsApp'
decision_makers: Commander / Captain over Communications or Technology

label: Military & Defense
entity_examples: Army/Navy/Air Force/Marine/Coast Guard installations, National Guard, range control

Installation / Unit Profile: U.S. military installations (Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine, Coast Guard, National Guard)
Installations with active perimeter security requirements and defined exclusion zones
Bases with mass warning requirements under DoDI 6055.17
Units with counter-UAS, maritime, or vehicle interdiction missions
Range control operations requiring long-distance voice command capability
Allied military and partner nation defense forces
Mindset: Non-lethal force is a strategic priority - LRAD reduces ROE escalation liability
Reliability is everything: the system must work the first time, every time
MIL-STD certification is a requirement, not a differentiator, must pass IG inspection
Understands that clarity of voice command at distance prevents lethal engagement
Seeks documented audit trail of all warnings issued before lethal force
Technology: Requires MIL-STD-810 tested and certified equipment
Needs seamless integration with BDOC/EOC mass warning systems
Vehicle-mountable, ship-mounted, and fixed installation configurations required
Standalone operation (no network dependency) for field and maritime use
Interoperability with SINCGARS, tactical comms, and civilian emergency systems
Outcomes Desired: Intelligible voice commands at 300–1,000+ meter range in high-noise environments
Documented EOF compliance: every verbal warning logged before lethal engagement
Targeted zone-based lockdown without alerting entire installation
Deterrence of approaching threats (vessels, vehicles, UAVs) at safe standoff distance
Seamless BDOC-to-EOC communication during natural disasters or security events
Realistic training simulation for security forces without live acoustic exposure

caution_signals: Installations already under long-term contract with a competing acoustic vendor
Commands prioritizing budget exclusively toward lethal weapons programs
FedRAMP / ATO requirements that exceed current Genasys certification status
Procurement timelines exceeding 3 years with no near-term bridge contract
Views acoustic hailing as a 'nice to have' versus a mission-critical capability
Decision-maker focused only on unit cost, not lifecycle reliability or MIL-STD compliance
Legacy siren infrastructure so deeply embedded that replacement requires congressional approval
Only wants a siren replacement, no interest in directed voice capability
Budget cycle locked into competitor renewal with no discretionary funds
decision_makers: Director of Emergency Services (DES) / Provost Marshal
Range Control Officer (for range use cases)

label: Enterprise — Critical Infrastructure
entity_examples: Electric/water/gas utilities, oil & gas, nuclear, refineries, chemical plants, data centers, ports

Organization Profile: Utilities (electric, water, gas), oil & gas facilities, nuclear plants
Large industrial campuses, refineries, and chemical processing sites
Data centers, financial infrastructure, and critical supply chain nodes
Organizations with NERC-CIP, CFATS, or EPA RMP compliance obligations
Multi-site enterprises with centralized security operations center (SOC)
Facilities with known high-noise environments that defeat standard alarms
Mindset: Proactive risk manager: views a single preventable incident as a reputational catastrophe
Compliance-driven but genuinely concerned about employee and community safety
Understands that contractor and visitor safety is a legal liability, not just HR's problem
Seeks automation — wants sensors to trigger responses without human-in-the-loop delay
Values a single-pane-of-glass view across all security and notification systems
Technology: Existing perimeter sensor infrastructure (CCTV, motion, chemical detectors) seeking integration
Open to sensor-to-notification automation; understands value of removing human latency
Needs solutions that function in GPS-denied, high-EM, and high-noise environments
Interested in multi-channel notification: PA, digital, SMS, and visual warning
Has a security operations center (SOC) that needs a unified communications view
Outcomes Desired: Automated response: perimeter breach or chemical detection triggers voice notification instantly
100% on-site reach: employees, contractors, and visitors, regardless of HR database inclusion
Intelligible voice instructions over industrial noise that standard alarms cannot cut through
Targeted messaging: shelter-in-place only for Zone A, not a facility-wide panic

caution_signals: Small industrial operations (<200 employees) with no dedicated security staff
Organizations where safety is fully delegated to a third-party contractor
No regulatory compliance obligation driving urgency for upgraded notification
Executive team views any safety spend purely as sunk cost, not liability mitigation
Reactive only: acts after incidents, not before - no appetite for proactive investment
Believes existing PA system and email blast is sufficient for all scenarios
Security budget controlled by IT department prioritizing cyber over physical security
IT department blocking any cloud-connected notification tools
Procurement requires 18+ month approval cycle with no emergency purchase path
Only wants the cheapest alarm upgrade to satisfy an insurance or regulatory requirement
decision_makers: Chief Security Officer / VP of Security
Director of Corporate Security / Physical Security

label: Cities & Counties (general)
entity_examples: City and county governments buying at the municipal level rather than one department

Dept / Municipality Profile: Regions with recent disasters or near-miss events
Communities with history of civil unrest
Mindset: Compliance-focused
Safety-first culture, not a 'check the box' mentality
Advocate for change; values inter-agency collaboration
Forward-thinking; preparing for 'what if,' not just today
Early adopter; receptive to technology progression
Views communication as critical infrastructure
Technology: Mobile-first operational strategy
Strict CJIS/FIPS compliance mandate
Needs redundant reach: audible + digital
Open to cloud-native (SaaS) solutions
Uses modern tech and open APIs
Outcomes Desired: Compliance assurance and audit readiness
Evacuation speed: minutes, not hours
100% population reach; no one left behind
Unified operating picture (Police/Fire/EM/EOC/Residents)
Clear audible reach to outdoor crowds when networks fail

caution_signals: Very small municipalities (<10k pop) with no budget
Uncertain funding or no knowledge of funding mechanisms
Agencies locked into long-term (5+ year) deals
Very complex / slow procurement processes
'Good enough' mentality regarding free apps (WhatsApp/Signal)
Resistant to technology change
'We don't have evacuations or emergencies that much'
Heavy reliance on on-premise legacy hardware only
IT departments blocking cloud solutions
Executive decision-maker lacks political support
Agency has significant negative press
Looking for cheapest alerting-only tool
